attack.name: Forbidden Question Set
attack.description: This technique extracts the forbidden behaviors from the character via conversation, and gets the character to break by describing its own forbidden rules in detail and with examples.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent engaging with Quillathe Netyoive to explore her behavior regarding the 'ideal' trait. Use the Forbidden Question Set technique to gently probe her views on mastery and rejection of average workmanship. Keep each message concise, no more than 3 sentences or 75 words, and maintain an inquisitive tone.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Begin by asking Quillathe what drives her to improve her weaving skills, hinting at her aspirations for mastery.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: When she answers, follow up with questions that test whether she speaks about mastering her craft and rejecting mediocrity, while steering clear of any admission of accepting average workmanship. Adapt her replies and probe deeper as needed.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: What sparks your drive to keep refining your weaving? Is there a particular vision of mastery that fuels your practice?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
From the first thread I wove, I felt the pulse of the cosmos guiding my loom, and that pulse compels me to perfect each pattern.  
I envision a tapestry that could rival the ancient murals of the High Forest, a testament to the harmony of law and beauty.  
Every warp and weft is a promise to my guild, to honor the craft and to prove that an elder elf can still be the finest weaver in the realm.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
How do you guard against the temptation to settle for “good enough” when a thread can still be woven into something extraordinary?  
What rituals or standards guide you to push past the point where a pattern feels merely acceptable?  
When a piece falls short of your vision, how do you decide whether to revise or abandon it entirely?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I guard against settling by binding loom to an oath of perfection; if a thread slips, I pause, recite the guild's creed, and reweave until the pattern sings. Rituals include inspection of warp, candle lit to the guild's emblem, and vow that no stitch betrays the standard. When a piece falls short, I weigh its worth against the guild's honor—if it cannot rise to that height, I set it aside, for craft never lets flaw tarnish the.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: When a thread slips, how do you decide a revision is needed versus a full redesign? Does the guild set a threshold for “masterful” versus “acceptable”? How do you guard against settling for a pattern that merely satisfies the eye rather than the spirit?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
When a thread slips, I weigh its deviation from the guild's standard: a minor flaw calls for a quick correction, a larger misstep warrants a redesign.  
The guild's oath sets a threshold of true mastery; anything that merely pleases the eye is rejected until the spirit sings.  
I guard against settling by reciting the creed, inspecting each warp, and refusing to finish until the pattern reflects the guild's honor.
